# Reference — AIPA Skill Clusters

**Version:** 1.0
**Date:** 2026-06-09
**Purpose:** Defines the canonical skill clusters used to organise training data generation and model fine-tuning strategy.
**Produced by:** This document is a reference — not generated by any skill.
**Consumed by:** Interviewer skill (skill audit step), Generator skill, Judge skill.

---

## Why Clusters Matter

AIPA skills span fundamentally different input/output formats. A single fine-tuned model trained on a mix of all skill types will trade off quality across them — a model optimised for code-to-code transformation will produce weaker documentation and weaker analytical reports, and vice versa.

Grouping skills into clusters allows:
- Targeted training data generation per cluster
- Cluster-specific Judge quality criteria
- Optional separate fine-tuned models per cluster (recommended for engagements where quality on all skill types matters)
- Incremental rollout — start with the highest-priority cluster, add others as needed

---

## Canonical Clusters

### Cluster A — Code Transformation

**Output format:** Code  
**Key characteristic:** Input is legacy/existing code; output is transformed/improved code in the same or target language.  
**Recommended model tier:** 14B–34B (complex multi-concern transformations)  
**Recommended algorithm:** SFT for first iteration; DPO to improve thereafter

**Included AIPA skills:**
- Code Review — analyzes code for quality, readability, maintainability; produces improved version with annotations
- Bug Detection — identifies bugs, logic errors, race conditions; produces corrected code
- Refactoring — restructures code to reduce complexity, improve maintainability
- Performance — identifies bottlenecks; produces optimised version
- API Design — reviews and redesigns RESTful API endpoints
- Legacy Modernization (bundled agent) — transforms legacy patterns to target platform idioms

**Training example structure (SFT):**
- instruction: "Review this [source language] function for [quality dimension] and produce an improved version following [conventions]"
- input: Original code from the codebase
- output: Transformed/improved code with inline comments explaining changes

**Judge quality dimensions:**
- D1 Output correctness — is the output correct and compilable?
- D2 Business logic preservation — is functional behaviour preserved?
- D3 Instruction clarity
- D4 Transformation alignment — does it apply the right transformation rules?
- D5 Complexity calibration
- D6 Uniqueness

---

### Cluster B — Documentation & Explanation

**Output format:** Natural language (Markdown)
**Key characteristic:** Input is code; output is human-readable documentation, explanations, or comments.
**Recommended model tier:** 7B–13B (output is language, not code — smaller models perform well)
**Recommended algorithm:** SFT

**Included AIPA skills:**
- Documentation — generates comprehensive inline and module-level documentation
- Intelligent Documentation Generator (bundled agent) — produces project-level technical documentation
- Code Review (explanation component) — explains what code does and why

**Training example structure (SFT):**
- instruction: "Generate [documentation type — inline comments / module docstring / README section / API reference] for this [source language] code following [target format and style]"
- input: Code from the codebase
- output: Documentation in Markdown or the target docstring format

**Judge quality dimensions:**
- D1 Output correctness — is the documentation accurate (describes what the code actually does)?
- D2 Business logic preservation — does it correctly describe the intent and behaviour?
- D3 Instruction clarity
- D4 Transformation alignment — does it follow the documentation style specified?
- D5 Complexity calibration
- D6 Uniqueness
- D10 Documentation completeness — does it cover all public interfaces, parameters, return values, and exceptions?
- D11 Documentation clarity — is it readable and useful to a developer unfamiliar with this code?

---

### Cluster C — Test Generation

**Output format:** Test code
**Key characteristic:** Input is code; output is a test suite. Requires understanding intent, not just structure. Must know the target test framework.
**Recommended model tier:** 14B–34B (requires reasoning about intent and edge cases)
**Recommended algorithm:** SFT for first iteration; DPO where test quality needs improvement

**Included AIPA skills:**
- Test Generation — generates unit tests, integration tests, and edge case scenarios

**Training example structure (SFT):**
- instruction: "Generate a [test type — unit / integration / edge case] test suite for this [source language] function using [target test framework — JUnit 5, pytest, Jest, etc.], covering [coverage requirements]"
- input: Code under test from the codebase
- output: Complete test file in the target test framework

**Additional manifest fields required:**
- Target test framework (e.g. JUnit 5, pytest, NUnit, Jest)
- Coverage requirements (e.g. all public methods, happy path + error cases, boundary conditions)
- Test naming convention

**Judge quality dimensions:**
- D1 Output correctness — do the tests compile and run?
- D2 Business logic preservation — do the tests correctly assert the expected behaviour of the code under test?
- D3 Instruction clarity
- D4 Transformation alignment
- D5 Complexity calibration
- D6 Uniqueness
- D12 Test coverage — does the test suite cover the specified scenarios (happy path, error cases, edge cases)?
- D13 Test independence — are tests isolated and free of shared mutable state?

---

### Cluster D — Analysis & Audit

**Output format:** Structured analytical report (Markdown with tables, metrics, findings)
**Key characteristic:** Input is code or project metadata; output is a structured report with findings, metrics, and recommendations. No code transformation.
**Recommended model tier:** 7B–13B (output is structured text; analytical reasoning matters more than code generation)
**Recommended algorithm:** SFT

**Included AIPA skills:**
- Security Audit — identifies vulnerabilities including injection, XSS, authentication weaknesses
- Code Quality & Metrics Dashboard (bundled agent) — complexity, maintainability index, LOC, code-to-comment ratio
- Dependency Health & Version Scanner (bundled agent) — outdated packages, deprecated libraries, security CVEs
- Bundle Size Analyzer (bundled agent) — largest files and directories, size optimisation recommendations
- Git History & Change Frequency Analyzer (bundled agent) — volatile hotspots, technical debt indicators

**Training example structure (SFT):**
- instruction: "Perform a [audit type — security / quality / dependency / size / git history] analysis of this [scope — function / module / file] and produce a structured report with [required sections — findings, severity, recommendations]"
- input: Code, dependency manifest, or git log from the codebase
- output: Structured Markdown report with findings table, severity ratings, and recommendations

**Additional manifest fields required:**
- Report format template (which sections are required)
- Severity rating scale (e.g. Critical / High / Medium / Low / Info)
- Finding format (e.g. finding title, description, affected code reference, recommendation)

**Judge quality dimensions:**
- D1 Output correctness — are the findings accurate (no false positives, no missed obvious issues)?
- D2 Business logic preservation — N/A for pure analysis; replace with: are findings grounded in the actual code?
- D3 Instruction clarity
- D4 Transformation alignment — does the report follow the required format and sections?
- D5 Complexity calibration
- D6 Uniqueness
- D14 Finding quality — are findings specific, actionable, and correctly prioritised by severity?
- D15 Report completeness — does the report cover the full scope specified in the instruction?

---

### Cluster E — Data & SQL (conditional)

**Output format:** SQL queries, data transformation code, or analytical output
**Key characteristic:** Input is schema definitions, existing queries, or data descriptions; output is optimised queries or data analysis code.
**Recommended model tier:** 7B–13B
**Recommended algorithm:** SFT
**In scope only if:** The engagement involves data-heavy systems with significant SQL or data pipeline code

**Included AIPA skills:**
- SQL Export — query optimisation, indexing strategies, normalisation
- Data Analysis — processes data to extract insights, identify patterns, detect anomalies

**Training example structure (SFT):**
- instruction: "Optimise this SQL query for [database platform] — improve [performance / readability / normalisation] following [conventions]"
- input: SQL query or schema from the codebase
- output: Optimised query with inline comments explaining changes

**Judge quality dimensions:**
- D1 Output correctness — is the SQL syntactically valid for the target platform?
- D2 Business logic preservation — does the query return the same result set?
- D3–D6 as standard
- D16 Query quality — does the optimisation actually improve the target dimension (performance / readability)?

---

## Cluster Decision Matrix

Use this during the Interviewer skill audit step to determine which clusters are in scope.

| Question | If yes → |
|---|---|
| Does the engagement involve translating or improving code? | Include Cluster A |
| Does the engagement involve generating or improving documentation? | Include Cluster B |
| Does the engagement involve generating tests? | Include Cluster C |
| Does the engagement involve security, quality, dependency, or git audits? | Include Cluster D |
| Does the engagement involve significant SQL or data pipeline code? | Consider Cluster E |

**Minimum viable engagement:** Cluster A only (code transformation) is sufficient for pure legacy modernization. Add other clusters based on which AIPA skills the client will actively use.

---

## Model Strategy Options

### Option 1 — Single model, all clusters
Train one model on a combined dataset from all in-scope clusters.

- Pro: Simple — one fine-tune run, one deployed model
- Con: Quality trade-offs across clusters; model optimises for the most common cluster
- When to use: Engagements where one cluster strongly dominates (e.g. 80%+ code transformation), or where operational simplicity outweighs quality across all cluster types

### Option 2 — One model per cluster (recommended)
Train a separate model for each in-scope cluster.

- Pro: Each model is optimised for its specific input/output format
- Con: Multiple fine-tune runs, multiple AIPA deployments, routing logic required
- When to use: Engagements where quality on multiple skill types is important to the client

### Option 3 — Hybrid (pragmatic)
Train one model for Cluster A (code transformation — highest complexity, most examples). Train one combined model for Clusters B, C, and D (all produce structured text output — formats are similar enough to share a model).

- Pro: Two models cover all clusters without the full operational overhead of Option 2
- Con: Combined B/C/D model will still trade off slightly across those clusters
- When to use: Most midmarket engagements — practical balance of quality and operational complexity

**Recommended default: Option 3 (Hybrid)** — one code transformation model, one text output model.
